title: Product Emergency Process — Premium Stakeholder Edition
description: A comprehensive, image-rich walkthrough of how Moonfare handles product emergencies. Built for stakeholders who need to know what qualifies, who runs the response, what to do (and not do), and how the lifecycle closes with a blameless post-mortem. Sourced directly from the Moonfare Emergency Playbook on Confluence.
author: Moonfare Product Operations

modules.title: Module 1 — What Is a Product Emergency?
modules.description: Threshold criteria, common categories, and the calibration mindset.

content.content:
Stakeholder training · v3.0

Product
Emergency Process

Recognize the signal. Escalate fast. Coordinate cleanly. Recover with discipline.

A Stakeholder's Guide to Crisis Response at Moonfare

checklistItems.title: Recognize when an issue may be a product emergency
checklistItems.description: Apply the threshold criteria with confidence.

checklistItems.title: Escalate through the correct channels
checklistItems.description: PagerDuty, Jira, #emergency_stakeholders — never DMs.

checklistItems.title: Understand the L1/L2/L3 response structure
checklistItems.description: Know who's driving, supporting, and watching.

checklistItems.title: Follow updates in the right places
checklistItems.description: Single source of truth in Jira; status in Slack.

checklistItems.title: Support handling without disrupting resolution
checklistItems.description: Provide impact context; don't open parallel threads.

checklistItems.title: Understand the post-mortem
checklistItems.description: Blameless review — learning, not blame.

content.content: A product emergency is triggered when core functionality is broken, a large share of users is affected, revenue-impacting journeys are blocked, or critical product/data behavior is failing. The label changes the response — different cadence, different roles, different stakes.

data.pqQuote: If the issue affects core customer experience, critical operations, or revenue-generating workflows — assume it may be an emergency and escalate quickly. Speed beats certainty.

content.content: Any one of these flips the switch

data.cmpTitle: An issue becomes an Emergency when…

cmpColumns.title: ≥ 50% affected
cmpColumns.subtitle: or ≤ 20% can access a critical function

content.content: Tap a card to see what it looks like in practice.

flipCards.back: Investor sees a 500 error trying to view an allocation. The app errors out, no graceful fallback. Affects every user on that page.

flipCards.front: App not functional for a key flow
flipCards.back: The 'Sign agreement' button does nothing. Investors can browse but cannot complete commitments. Revenue path blocked.
Payments service returns 503 across all environments. Frontend keeps trying. Users see endless loading spinners.
The KYC provider goes down. New investors cannot complete verification. Pipeline stalls until provider recovers.
Primary DB cluster is unreachable. Reads fail, writes queue up. Every product surface degrades simultaneously.
An investor sees another investor's documents in their portal. Even one occurrence is treated as an emergency until contained.

data.layoutText:
Most product issues are not emergencies. They're bugs, papercuts, or feature requests. Emergencies are a small subset.

When in doubt: escalate. False positives are recoverable. Silence on a real emergency is not.

content.content: When you can't tell — escalate. The L1 will downgrade to a normal incident if it doesn't qualify. Far better than missing a real one.

questions.text: Which of the following is most likely to qualify as a product emergency?

options.text: A typo on a non-critical page
A critical investor journey is broken
One internal report is delayed by five minutes

questions.explanation: A broken critical investor journey blocks revenue and core customer experience — primary Emergency criteria.

questions.text: An issue affecting 10% of users with a known workaround is automatically a Product Emergency.
questions.explanation: It's likely a normal incident, not an Emergency. Threshold is ~50% impact OR ≤20% can access a critical function — and a known workaround typically means it's not blocking revenue.

questions.text: When in doubt about whether an issue is an emergency, you should…

options.text: Wait and see if it gets worse
Ask a colleague casually in DM
Escalate — the L1 will reclassify if needed
Open a Jira bug ticket and assign to engineering

questions.explanation: Speed beats certainty. False positives downgrade easily; missed emergencies don't.

modules.title: Module 2 — Who Does What?
modules.description: L1, L2, L3 escalation tiers and stakeholder responsibilities.

content.content:
Module 2

Who runs
the response

L1 · L2 · L3 · Stakeholders. Four roles. Four jobs. Zero ambiguity.

slides.title: L1, L2, L3 — three escalation tiers

content.content: Every emergency is staffed by these three role types working in parallel.

data.cmpTitle: From front-line intake to senior decision-making

cmpColumns.title: Incident Manager
cmpColumns.subtitle: Product Operations · Lily · Hasan · rotational PMs

bullets.text: Activated when L2 needs senior decisions

tabItems.title: L1 — Incident Manager
tabItems.content:
Lily, Hasan + rotational PMs from Product Ops.

L1 is the conductor — they don't fix the bug, they make sure the right people are fixing it and that everyone else knows what's going on. They're live for the duration: classifying, escalating, posting the 30-min Jira update, and shepherding the ticket through to a written post-mortem 10 working days later.

tabItems.title: L2 — SMEs
tabItems.content:
Engineering domain owners.

L2 is the muscle. They look at the metrics, read the code, and decide what to do. They have authority to ship a fix, disable a feature, or roll back a deploy without asking permission — but they'll loop in L3 the moment the scope or stakes grow beyond their authority.

tabItems.title: L3 — Senior Escalation
tabItems.content:
VP Engineering, Security, occasionally the CTO.

L3 only joins when something needs to escalate beyond the team handling it. That might be a regulator notification, a public communication, an architectural decision under pressure, or simply that an L2 team is stuck. They are the deciders of last resort.

tabItems.title: Stakeholders
tabItems.content:
You.

Your job is to report fast, follow updates, and stay out of the way. The clearest contribution you can make is a precise impact statement: who is affected, what is broken, when it started. After that, monitor the Jira ticket and the stakeholder Slack channel — don't open parallel threads.

data.pqQuote: In an emergency, ambiguity is the enemy. Everyone needs to know — within sixty seconds — whether they're driving, supporting, or watching. Get that wrong and the response slows to a crawl.

content.content: Drag each item to the role that owns it

cardSortCards.text: Reads the code, applies the fix
Reports the issue from the field
Decides to disable a feature flag

questions.text: Who is primarily responsible for coordinating communication and managing the emergency process?
questions.explanation: L1 owns the process from intake through post-mortem. Per the Emergency Playbook.

questions.text: Match each role to its primary job

matchingPairs.right: Coordinate the response and own communication
Investigate, scope, and apply the technical fix
Report impact and follow updates without disrupting

questions.explanation: Each role is mutually exclusive — overlap creates ambiguity.

questions.text: Stakeholders should attempt ad-hoc fixes if they have access and the issue looks contained.
questions.explanation: Never. Even a 'small' independent action can mask the root cause or duplicate work the response team is already doing.

modules.title: Module 3 — End-to-End Emergency Process
modules.description: Six steps from alert to post-mortem.

content.content:
Module 3

From signal
to post-mortem

Six steps. Same arc, every time.

timelineEvents.title: Alert or Report Raised
timelineEvents.description: PagerDuty page, internal stakeholder escalation, Product Ops observation, or pattern across multiple tickets.

timelineEvents.title: Acknowledge & Confirm
timelineEvents.description: Acknowledge in PagerDuty, respond to ticket, confirm whether the issue qualifies as an emergency.

timelineEvents.title: Create Coordination Space
timelineEvents.description: Use the emergency workflow to spin up the Slack coordination channel. Add L2 responders. Start the meeting.

timelineEvents.title: Investigate & Stabilise
timelineEvents.description: Assess impact. Define next actions. Document in Jira/PagerDuty. Apply mitigations: feature flags, banners, infra/security as needed.

timelineEvents.title: Communicate · Every 30 min
timelineEvents.description: Stakeholder-facing updates on the ticket every 30 minutes until resolution. Standard templates.

timelineEvents.title: Resolve & Close
timelineEvents.description: Communicate completed actions. Close Jira ticket. Send stakeholder email. Create post-mortem doc. Hold the review.

content.content: Click any pin to see what the response team is monitoring during a live emergency.

hotspotMarkers.label: Service health
hotspotMarkers.description: Live charts for the affected service. P50/P99 latency, error rate, throughput.

hotspotMarkers.label: Jira ticket
hotspotMarkers.description: Single source of truth. Every update, every action, every decision lands here.

hotspotMarkers.label: Slack coordination
hotspotMarkers.description: Real-time backchannel for the response team. Stakeholders watch the read-only stakeholder channel.

hotspotMarkers.label: Action timer
hotspotMarkers.description: 30-minute update cadence. L1 posts on the ticket regardless of whether there's news.

data.scenarioTitle: It's 18:30 on a Friday. An investor at a partner bank tells you the 'Sign agreement' button does nothing. They've tried twice. Three of their colleagues report the same. What do you do?
data.scenarioDescription: Use the playbook. No ad-hoc fixes.

choices.text: Try it yourself first to confirm
choices.feedback: Reasonable to verify, but quick — don't burn 10 minutes.
Good — fast escalation. The L1 will validate.

choices.text: DM your VP to ask if it's a known issue
choices.feedback: Side-channel. This fragments the response and slows you down.

scenarioSteps.text: Your VP is in a meeting. 8 minutes lost. The investor's colleague has now also tried 3 times. What now?

choices.text: Post immediately to #emergency_stakeholders with the impact
choices.feedback: Right call — late but correct. The lesson: skip the side-channel next time.
No. You're now actively withholding signal during a likely revenue-blocking incident.

scenarioSteps.text: L1 confirms it's an Emergency. They ask for impact context. You provide…

choices.text: 'I think a few investors can't sign.'
choices.feedback: Too vague. The L1 needs specifics.

choices.text: '4 investors at Bank X confirmed, all on Sign-Agreement step. First report 18:22, last 18:34.'
Forward the investor's email screenshots without context
choices.feedback: Raw data without synthesis slows the L1 down.

scenarioSteps.text: L1 asks again for specifics. You re-collect from your contacts and send: 4 investors, Bank X, Sign-Agreement, 18:22-18:34. What now?

choices.feedback: Good recovery. Lesson: lead with specifics next time.

scenarioSteps.text: It's now 18:55. The Bank's relationship manager DMs you directly: 'Is this fixed? My CEO wants to know.' What do you do?

choices.text: Reply with the latest from the Jira ticket
choices.feedback: Wrong. Direct partner contact during incidents must go through the Partnerships relay.

choices.text: Forward the question to Max & Nicolai (Partnerships)
choices.feedback: Correct. Even with the best intentions, direct stakeholder→partner replies fragment the response.

scenarioSteps.endMessage: Textbook handling. You escalated fast, gave the L1 useful context, and routed the partner question through the right channel.
The Bank's CEO got two slightly different stories — one from you, one from Partnerships — and the relationship took a hit that lasted weeks. The fix took 35 minutes. The relationship took 6 months.
The L1 found out about the issue 22 minutes later than they should have, from a different stakeholder. Resolution was delayed. Don't withhold signal during an emergency.

questions.text: How often should updates be posted during an active emergency?
questions.explanation: 30 minutes — even if there's no news. Silence is read as the response stalling.

questions.text: Put these emergency-process steps in order
questions.explanation: The six-step arc is fixed: detect → ack → coordinate → investigate → comms → resolve.

questions.text: The standard stakeholder-facing update cadence during a live emergency is every ____ minutes.
questions.explanation: 30-min cadence is non-negotiable for the duration of a Tier-1 emergency.

modules.title: Module 4 — Channels and Tools
modules.description: Where to follow updates, where to post, and the one rule about partner relays.

content.content: Three channels. Each has a single audience and a single purpose. Get the channel wrong and the message gets lost.

tableRows: Reach a partner about the incident
Never DM partners directly during a live incident

content.content: Stakeholders should follow the Jira ticket and designated Slack updates rather than opening multiple side conversations. Side conversations fragment decision-making and increase confusion.

modules.title: Module 5 — What Stakeholders Should Do
modules.description: The Do / Do-Not list, with examples.

content.content: A short list of habits that separate helpful stakeholders from accidental disrupters.

data.cmpTitle: Two columns. Strong opinions on each.

bullets.text: Raise issues fast when impact is suspected
Attempt ad-hoc fixes outside the process
Resend affected data/documents on your own
Start duplicate tickets or parallel threads
DM partners about the incident directly

slides.title: What a great escalation looks like

data.layoutText:
Bad:

"I think a few investors can't sign."

Good:

4 investors at Bank X, all on the Sign-Agreement step, 18:22–18:34 CET. Three retried twice. Bank RM aware.

content.content: Specifics save 20 minutes of back-and-forth. Specifics let the L1 classify in one read. Aim for that quality every time.

questions.text: Where should stakeholders follow official emergency updates?

options.text: Jira ticket and designated emergency channels

questions.explanation: Jira is the source of truth; #emergency_stakeholders mirrors key updates. DMs and email fragment the response.

questions.text: If a stakeholder has technical access and can probably fix it, they should do so to save time.
questions.explanation: Never. Independent action masks root cause and duplicates work. Even technically-capable stakeholders should escalate first.

questions.text: What information should a stakeholder provide when escalating an emergency?
questions.explanation: Specifics: who is affected (count, segment, identifying detail), what is broken (the exact action or surface), when it started (timestamp), how it was reported (so the L1 can verify).

modules.title: Module 6 — Special Case: Data Breach
modules.description: When data is at stake, the rules tighten. Stop, contain, escalate.

content.content: A different decision tree, with one strict rule about independent action.

data.layoutText:
Through Product — if the issue happened in the portal or Moonfare systems, or Engineering needs to investigate or fix something.

Direct to Compliance — if it's purely a manual or process issue, no system involvement.

When in doubt: assume Product. The L1 will hand off to Compliance if it doesn't involve systems.

checklistItems.title: Stop and contain the issue
checklistItems.description: Don't propagate. Don't resend. Lock down access if you can.

checklistItems.title: Create a Product emergency ticket
checklistItems.description: Use the standard template. Tag it as breach.

checklistItems.title: Notify in Slack
checklistItems.description: #emergency_stakeholders and follow the L1 from there.

checklistItems.title: Stay available for questions
checklistItems.description: Compliance and Security may need follow-ups quickly.

checklistItems.title: Do not independently fix or resend
checklistItems.description: This is the firmest rule. Independent action can compound the breach.

content.content: Even if you know exactly how to fix it. Even if it's small. Even if you're trying to help. Stop. Contain. Escalate. The L1 + Compliance + Security will run the playbook.

accordionItems.title: Immediate actions taken
accordionItems.content: Anything you did to contain the issue (e.g. revoked a session, removed a document from a portal, paused a feed). Document before the response team needs it.

accordionItems.title: Suspected classification
accordionItems.content: What kind of breach do you suspect? Document misrouting? Cross-tenant data leak? Unauthorised access? It's OK to be uncertain — just say so.

accordionItems.title: Links and attachments
accordionItems.content: Email screenshots (with sensitive info redacted), Jira ticket links, document IDs, time-stamps. The more concrete, the faster Compliance can assess.

accordionItems.title: Who knows
accordionItems.content: List who you've already told, including external parties. This shapes Compliance's notification plan.

questions.text: In a suspected product-related data breach, what should stakeholders avoid?
questions.explanation: Independent fixes or resends can compound the breach. Stop, contain, escalate — that's it.

questions.text: If a data issue is purely manual/process and doesn't involve Moonfare systems, it goes directly to Compliance, not Product.
questions.explanation: Per the Data Breach & Misrouted Documents procedure. Product is only the entry point when systems are involved.

modules.title: Module 7 — After the Incident
modules.description: The post-mortem and the culture that makes it work.

content.content: Resolution is not the end. The post-mortem is where the next incident gets prevented.

data.pqQuote: Trust is built in seconds. It's lost in minutes. Our post-mortems are blameless because the next emergency is more likely to be caught by the engineer who already learned the hard way — not the one who's hiding from the fallout.

timelineEvents.title: Resolution Confirmed
timelineEvents.description: L1 sends the all-clear on #emergency_stakeholders. Jira ticket closes.

timelineEvents.title: Stakeholder Email
timelineEvents.description: Summary email to all stakeholders with timeline, impact, and root cause (preliminary).

timelineEvents.title: Post-Mortem Doc Created
timelineEvents.description: L1 starts the document. SMEs fill in technical detail. Findings, action items, owners.

timelineEvents.title: Post-Mortem Review
timelineEvents.description: Open meeting. All stakeholders welcome. Blameless format — what failed, what worked, what changes.

timelineEvents.title: Post-Mortem Published
timelineEvents.description: Document linked from the Confluence Emergencies index. Action items tracked separately.

content.content: After every emergency, four areas get reviewed

checklistItems.title: Process
checklistItems.description: Were the right people in the room? Did the cadence work?

checklistItems.title: Tooling
checklistItems.description: Did monitoring catch it? Did the escalation tooling work?

checklistItems.title: Communication
checklistItems.description: Were updates clear? Did stakeholders feel informed?

checklistItems.title: Prevention
checklistItems.description: What change ships now to prevent recurrence?

content.content: Mock emergencies and refresher trainings happen on a regular cadence. Stakeholders are part of these — your honest feedback is what improves the process.

modules.title: Module 8 — Final Assessment & Quick Reference
modules.description: Comprehensive check + a printable quick-reference card.

content.content: Eight questions across all seven modules. Pass mark: 70%.

questions.text: Which of the following is most likely to qualify as a product emergency?

options.text: A typo on a non-critical page
A critical investor journey is broken
One internal report is delayed by five minutes

questions.explanation: Confluence: Emergency Playbook — broken critical journeys are textbook Tier-1.

questions.text: Where should stakeholders follow official emergency updates?

options.text: Jira ticket and designated emergency channels

questions.explanation: Confluence: Emergency Playbook — Jira is source of truth, Slack is mirror.

questions.text: How often should updates be posted during an active emergency?
questions.explanation: 30-minute cadence is non-negotiable.

questions.text: Who is primarily responsible for coordinating communication and managing the emergency process?
questions.explanation: L1 owns the process from intake through post-mortem.

questions.text: In a suspected product-related data breach, what should stakeholders avoid?
questions.explanation: Stop. Contain. Escalate. No independent action.

questions.text: Name 3 examples of incidents that may qualify as a product emergency.
questions.explanation: Acceptable answers include: app crash, key flow not functional, backend service failure, third-party outage affecting product, database outage, data breach, ≥50% users affected, ≤20% can access a critical function.

questions.text: What information should a stakeholder provide when escalating an emergency?
questions.explanation: Who is affected (count, segment), what is broken (exact action/surface), when it started (timestamp), and how it was reported. Specificity speeds the L1's classification.

questions.text: What happens after an emergency is resolved?
questions.explanation: Stakeholder email + closed Jira ticket + post-mortem document + blameless review meeting within ~5 working days, doc published within ~10 working days. Process/tooling/comms/prevention reviewed.

checklistItems.title: Escalate immediately
checklistItems.description: Speed beats certainty. False positives downgrade easily.

checklistItems.title: Ensure a Jira emergency ticket exists
checklistItems.description: Single source of truth from minute one.

checklistItems.title: Follow the official emergency channel
checklistItems.description: #emergency_stakeholders for status.

checklistItems.title: Provide business impact details
checklistItems.description: Who, what, when. Specifics.

checklistItems.title: Wait for coordinated updates from the incident owner
checklistItems.description: Don't open parallel threads.

data.pqQuote: Fast escalation. Single source of truth. Clear ownership. Disciplined communication. That's the entire playbook in one line.

content.content: Source references: Emergency Playbook · Mobile Emergency Process · Data Breach & Misrouted Documents Procedure · Emergency Refresher · Weekly Stakeholder Meeting · Weekly Prod Ops Sync
